question: Read the story and answer whether the following statement is true or false. The people convinced the King to make a proclamation.
answer:
This statement is false.

The king made a proclamation because he was convinced that his thoughts were right.

question: Read the story and answer whether the following statement is true or false. The hermit spoke usually to everyone.
answer:
This statement is false.
Explanation:
This line uses Hyperbole because it makes an intentional, sweeping exaggeration (“no one" over the “whole earth") to emphasize the complete lack of social anxiety and unhappiness in the animal kingdom.

question: Write down in your notebook two points for the following. How do you know the learned advisers who came to the court confused the king?
answer:
In response to the question of who the king needed most, the advisers answered that it was the councillors, the priests, the doctors, and the warriors, but not a single answer was unanimous.
In response to the king’s question about which was the most important occupation, the advisers gave varied responses, such as science, skill in warfare, and religious worship.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Write down in your notebook two points for the following. How do you know the king was humble?
answer:
The king disguised himself in simple clothes and went to meet the hermit. The latter met with only common people.
Even though the hermit did not answer the king, the king felt obliged and offered help.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Write down in your notebook two points for the following. How do you know the king’s enemy was repentant?
answer:
When the king’s enemy woke up the next morning, he spontaneously asked for forgiveness from the king.
The enemy recounted his entire story of revenge and promised the king that, since he had saved his life, he would serve him for the rest of his life.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Write down in your notebook two points for the following. How do you know the hermit was truly wise?
answer:
The hermit did not give the king easy answers, but rather tested the king’s patience. It is a characteristic of a truly wise woman.
The hermit related every experience that the king underwent since his arrival at the hermit’s hut to the time he forgave his enemy and satisfactorily answered the three questions of the king.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: The learned people were sometimes divided in their opinions, different persons giving quite different answers; at other times, none of them gave an answer.
answer:
n example of the learned people being divided in opinion and providing different answers was when some said that science was the most important occupation, while others said that it was warfare, and still others said that it was religion.
There is also an instance of the learned people not giving an exact answer but instead suggesting ways to find one. It can be seen when they suggest that it is impossible to find the best time for every action, and thus, the king should have a group of wise councillors, who could help him fix the best time for every action.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Though the hermit did not say anything to the king for some time, he did not ignore the king or treat him rudely in any way. Do you agree? What evidence of his politeness can you point out?
answer: Yes, I do agree that though the hermit didn’t say anything to the king for some time, he didn’t ignore the king or treat him rudely in any way, especially as he responded to his questions through his actions. The fact that he stopped digging to listen to the king and willingly gave the spade to the king when the king suggested he dig for a while shows that the hermit was polite. Moreover, it shows that he was responding to the king’s words, as he knowingly continued to dig so that the king would offer his help and later realise the meaning behind his actions. Even when the king lost his patience, the hermit calmly replied and asked the king to look at the wounded man running towards them. These instances are evidence that not only was the hermit polite to the king, but he also responded to the king’s words.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: The hermit ‘spoke only to common people’; so the king ‘put on simple clothes’. Do you think the king hoped to be mistaken for a common man, or was he just showing that he was a humble person?
answer: I think the king wanted to show he was humble because he was desperate to get answers to his questions. In fact, he approached the hermit and immediately sought answers to his queries. He even lost patience when the hermit didn’t answer his questions for a long time shows that the king didn’t expect to be treated as a common man. Instead of trying to learn from the hermit’s actions, the king was only interested in finding his own answers, which shows that, though he dressed in simple clothes, he still expected to be respected as a king. On the other hand, the hermit’s constant ignorance of the king’s repeated questions shows that he knew he was not a common man. The king’s impatience must also have made the hermit realise that he wasn’t dealing with a common man. His firm resolve to make the king understand the answers to his questions shows that he wanted to treat him like any other man, so that he could make him more humble.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Did the king behave as an ordinary person, rather than as a ruler, at the hermit’s hut? What shows it? Did he also act as a good, kind person? When did he do so?
answer: Initially, the king didn’t behave as an ordinary man. We understand this when he straightaway asked the hermit to answer his questions. When he saw the old hermit trying to dig the ground for a long time, he felt sorry for him and helped him out of kindness. However, he still intended to get an answer from the hermit and thus lost his patience when the hermit didn’t reply, even after he had helped him dig for almost the entire day. The king showed generosity when he stayed and nursed the wounded man back to health and forgave him when the man revealed that he was his enemy, who had come to kill him. These actions of the king were completely selfless and showed that deep down, even he was a kind and humble man.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Do you think the hermit knew, beforehand, not only about the king’s arrival but about the ambush by his enemy? Think a little about this and say what you really feel.
answer: Yes, I think that the hermit might have known, beforehand, not only about the king’s arrival, but also about the ambush by his enemy. The king was a well-known and popular figure, and many people knew he was on a quest to find answers to his three questions. If the king decided to journey to the forest outside his city, the news might have spread, and the hermit might have gotten wind of it. Also, the enemy was hiding around the hermit’s hut. Since the hermit had lived there for a long time, he must have known every inch of his surroundings and would have figured out immediately if someone had entered the surroundings. Thus, in my opinion, the hermit was aware of the king’s arrival and the enemy, who was in hiding, beforehand.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Consider this list of the different things that happened and rearrange them in the order of time, that is, what happened first, what happened next, and so on.
answer:
c. The king executed the bearded man’s brother.
a. The bearded man resolved to kill the king.
e. The bearded man laid an ambush to kill the king.
b. The king went alone to see the hermit.
g. The bearded man came out of the ambush.
f. The king’s bodyguards recognised and wounded the bearded man.
d. The king spent the night at the hermit’s hut.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Read the story in your own language, summarize the following aspect of the story in 4 to 5 lines in your own language. Write it in your notebook. King’s problem
answer:
राजाला पडलेले प्रश्‍न (King’s problem):
एखादे काम करण्यासाठी सर्वांत योग्य वेळ कोणती? कोणती व्यक्‍ती माझ्यासाठी सर्वांत महत्त्वाची असली पाहिजे, की ज्यामुळे मी तिच्याकडे जास्त लक्ष दिले पाहिजे? आणि कोणती गोष्ट करण्यास मी प्रथम प्राधान्य द्यावे? हे तीन प्रश्‍न राजाच्या मनात निर्माण झाले होते. त्या प्रश्‍नांची उत्तरे शोधण्याचा तो प्रयत्न करत होता.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Read the story in your own language, summarize the following aspect of the story in 4 to 5 lines in your own language. Write it in your notebook. Attempts made to find a solution
answer:
प्रश्‍नांची उत्तरे शोधण्यासाठी त्याने केलेले प्रयत्न (Attempts made to find a solution):
आपल्याला पडलेल्या प्रश्‍नांची उत्तरे शोधण्यासाठी राजाने आपल्या राज्यात दवंडी पिटवली आणि जो कोणी त्याला पडलेल्या प्रश्‍नांची योग्य उत्तरे देईल त्याला मोठे बक्षीस देण्यात येईल असे त्याने जाहीर केले. अनेक विद्वान लोकांनी दरबारात येऊन राजाच्या प्रश्‍नांची वेगवेगळी उत्तरे दिली; मात्र ती उत्तरे ऐकून राजाचे समाधान झाले नाही. त्यानंतर त्याने आपल्या प्रश्‍नांची उत्तरे शोधण्यासाठी जंगलात राहणाऱ्या एका प्रसिदध यतींची (ऋषीची) भेट घेण्याचे ठरवले.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Read the story in your own language, summarize the following aspect of the story in 4 to 5 lines in your own language. Write it in your notebook. Climax
answer:
कथेचा परमोच्च बिंदू (Climax):
आपल्याला पडलेल्या प्रश्‍नांची उत्तरे शोधण्यासाठी राजा जंगलात राहणाऱ्या विद्‌वान यतींकडे गेला. त्यावेळी तेथे त्याला एक जखमी माणूस दिसला. राजाने त्या माणसाची सेवा-सुश्रूषा केली. दिवसभराच्या धावपळीमुळे थकलेला राजा यतींच्या झोपड़ीमध्ये त्या जखमी व्यक्तीच्या शेजारीच झोपी गेला. सकाळी त्याला जाग आल्यावर त्या जखमी माणसाने राजाची माफी मागितली. आपण व्यक्तिगत वैमनस्यातून राजाला मारण्याच्या हेतूने आलो असल्याचे त्याने राजाला सांगितले. आपल्याला जीवानिशी मारायला आलेला माणूस एवढ्या सहजपणे आपला मित्र झाल्याचे जाणवून राजाला खूप आनंद झाला. हा या कथेचा परमोच्च बिंदू म्हणजेच Climax आहे.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Read the story in your own language, summarize the following aspect of the story in 4 to 5 lines in your own language. Write it in your notebook. Solution
answer:
प्रश्‍नांची उकल (Solution):
तुमच्या हातात असलेला आताचा क्षण म्हणजेच वर्तमानकाळ हा एखादे काम करण्यासाठी सर्वांत योग्य वेळ आहे. तुमच्या सोबत असलेली व्यक्‍ती ही तुमच्यासाठी सर्वांत महत्त्वाची असली पाहिजे. तुमच्या सोबत ज्या क्षणी जी व्यकती असेल तिच्या कल्याणाचे, भल्याचे काम करणे या गोष्टीस प्रथम प्राधान्य दिले पाहिजे. ही राजाच्या प्रश्‍नांना यतीने दिलेली उत्तरे आहेत.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Read the story in your own language, summarize the following aspect of the story in 4 to 5 lines in your own language. Write it in your notebook. Message
answer:
तात्पर्य (Message):
वर्तमानकाळ हाच कोणतीही गोष्ट करण्यास सर्वांत योग्य वेळ आहे. त्यावेळी तुमच्या सोबत ज्या व्यक्ती असतील त्यांच्याकडे तुम्ही नीट लक्ष दिले पाहिजे आणि त्यांचे कल्याण करण्यासाठी जे काही करणे आपल्या हाती आहे, ते सारे केले पाहिजे. असे वागल्यानेच आपले वर्तन आदर्श ठरेल.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Narrate an experience of your own that has helped you to realise that ‘Patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet.’ Write it in your notebook, in about 20 lines.
answer: I can never forget the day when I turned five. I still feel nostalgic when I remember that day, as it is etched in my mind forever. Since it was my birthday, my grandfather wanted to gift me something that was both priceless and timeless. A true farmer at heart, he took me to the corner of our garden and helped me plant a mango sapling. Each year we watched it grow, but I was eager to eat its fruit. My grandfather said that one has to have patience, as Patience is bitter, but its fruit is sweet. I did not realise its worth till the time my grandfather passed away and the tree bore fruit exactly 12 years later. As I bit into the first ripe mango fresh off the tree, it tasted heavenly, and I relished every bit of its pure sweetness. I also remembered my grandfather’s words, which will serve as a beacon in my life.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: After reading this story, develop a dialogue with 2 of your classmates about the characters in the story. Besides the tactful introduction to the conversation and write 8 to 10 sets of dialogues.
answer:
Yogesh:
Hey, Sonal! Will you play the part of the king in the play that the teacher taught us today?

Sonal:
Do you mean ‘The Three Questions’? Well, Yogesh, I haven’t seen you this enthusiastic about a play. Are you playing the hermit?

Yogesh:
Why, yes! I was initially upset when Ms Vijaya asked me to play the part, though.

Sonal:
Why? Is it because there are fewer dialogues?

Yogesh:
Yes. But I guess it would be a huge challenge to emote through the scenes with little to say, because, you see, the hermit is the hero of the play.

Sonal:
Indeed! The crux of the play is the interaction between the king and the hermit. So, I’m happy that I too get an important role in the play.

Pranit:
No, both of you have missed it completely. I, the enemy, am the vital element in the discovery of the king’s answers. Without me, the story is incomplete and meaningless.

Yogesh:
Sure! You are the extraordinary twist in the story. And Sonal, as I was saying, I love the ending. The hermit says so little, and yet the king gets his answers!

Sonal:
Leo Tolstoy is indeed a genius!

Pranit:
I totally agree with you!

Yogesh:
Why don’t we wait back after school today and practice?

Sonal:
Sure, count me in, guys.

Yogesh:
Ok then, let’s go to class and meet at 4:00 p.m. in the school auditorium.

Sonal:
Bye for now.

Yogesh:
Bye!

Pranit:
See you both at 4:00!
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: From the library or Internet, read the story ‘How much land does a man need?’ by Leo Tolstoy and write a review of the same, covering the following points. Background of the story Characters
answer:
Background of the story:
Leo Tolstoy wrote “How much land does a man need?” against the backdrop of the massive changes in 19th-century Russia. Until the emancipation of Russian serfs by Tsar Alexander II, the peasants were virtual slaves of landowners and aristocrats. Tolstoy wrote this story after the Serfs had already gained their freedom for 25 years. They now had rights and their own land. There was progress among the peasants, but Tolstoy was apprehensive about whether the peasants’ progress brought changes they would regret. This story serves as a harsh warning against unchecked materialism, clearly established through the fate of Pahom, the protagonist, and his sad, untimely death.
Characters:
Pahom (the protagonist), his wife, his sister-in-law, the Bashkirs, and the Devil.
Plot/Theme:
Pehom, a Russian peasant, overhears his wife and her sister arguing over whether it is better to live in the country or the city. This leads Pahom to make the dangerous declaration that if he had just enough land, he would not even fear the Devil. The Devil hears this boast, decides to put it to the test, and exploits Pahom’s greed. The story relates to Pahom’s success in buying land, yet also his dissatisfaction.
Climax:
Pahom learns about the Bashkir region, where very fertile land can be purchased cheaply. The Bashkirs agree to sell him for 1000 roubles, as much land as he can pace off in a day, as long as he returns before the end of the day to the starting point. Pahom walks far, trying to get as much land as possible, but when he sees more fertile land ahead of him, greed overtakes his senses, and he keeps walking further and further away without realising it would be difficult for him to return to the starting point. In his rush to get back to the starting point. In his rush to get back to the starting point, which he succeeds in, he collapses and dies. When he dies, the Baskhins ask Pahom’s servant to bury him on the same land, and he ends up with six feet of land, enough to bury him instead of all the land that he had acquired.
Message/Moral:
The story tells us about the destructive consequences of human ambition & greed. The message is clear: a warning against biting off more than you can chew. The story shows us how human nature pushes us to want more and more. We are never content, no matter how well off we may be. While trying to improve our standard of living, we risk ending up with nothing. It gives us the message that greed and excessive desire for earthly desires can destroy a person.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.2 - Three Questions

question: Complete the following table. ‘The Three Stories in the Life of Steve Jobs’
answer:
bout
Setbacks
Reactions
Achievements and benefits

First story
Connecting the dots
Dropped out of Reed College after six months; had to struggle to fulfil basic needs such as food, a place to stay, etc.
Took up the calligraphy class at Reed College and learned about serif and sans-serif typefaces and what enhances typography.
The calligraphy course helped design the Mac, as it included multiple typefaces and proportionally spaced fonts.

Second story
Love and loss
Being fired from Apple, which he had jointly started with Steve Wozniak.
Realised that he still loved what he did and decided to start all over again.
Started Next, Pixar and returned to Apple. 

Third story
Death
Diagnosed with a rare form of pancreatic cancer, he was on the verge of death.
Realised that one’s time is limited, so one must not waste it living on someone else’s terms, but rather listen. to one’s conscience; follow one’s heart and intuition and become what one truly wants to become.
Underwent surgery and recovered fully.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Say HOW? The calligraphy classes helped Steve Jobs after 10 years.
answer: 10 years later, when Steve Jobs was designing the first Macintosh computer, he recalled what he had learned in calligraphy classes about sans-serif typefaces and other elements of great typography. He designed all this into the Macintosh computer.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Say HOW? You can connect dots.
answer: We can’t connect dots by looking forward; we can only connect them by looking backwards. We have to trust that the dots will somehow connect in our destiny.
subject: English

question: Say HOW? Jobs reacted later on, after the shock of being fired from Apple.
answer: fter he had overcome the shock of being fired from Apple. Jobs felt the lightness of being a beginner again. He feels free to enter one of the most creative periods of his life. He started two companies - Next and Pixar.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Say HOW? Jobs was cured of a rare cancer.
answer: Jobs was diagnosed with a very rare form of pancreatic cancer. It was curable by surgery. Jobs underwent surgery and was cured.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Say HOW? Jobs acquired the famous words ‘Stay Hungry. Stay Foolish’
answer: When Jobs was young, he used to read an amazing publication called ‘The Whole Earth Catalogue’. The final issue was a photograph of an early morning country road beneath which were the words ‘Stay Hungry, Stay Foolish’. Steve acquired these words from the magazine.
subject: English
chapter: English Kumarbharati [English] Standard 10  chapter 2.3 - Connecting the Dots

question: Besides those given at the end of the talk by Steve Jobs, pick out other pieces of advice that Jobs gives in his speech. (a) ............... (b) ............... (c) ...............
answer:
You can’t connect the dots looking forward; you can only connect them looking backwards.
The only way to do great work is to love what you do.
If you haven’t found what you love, keep looking. Don’t settle.
